title: Database Cursors
text: Database Cursors Some DBMSs support a feature called cursors. A database cursor identifies the internal state of execution of a DML statement in the DBMS itself. Cursors are used to associate function calls via the API with a specific DML statement. Cursors also provide a bridge from the record-oriented 3GL programming language to the set-oriented query language. The DBMS connector can improve performance by maintaining a cache of cursors. In this way, a recurring query can be executed without being parsed again by the DBMS. A common technique in DBMS connectors for RDBMSs is to associate a standard set of cursors with every open table. By reserving one or more cursors for some connector requests, performance is significantly improved. Management of File and Cursor Resources Uniface guarantees that no run-time violation will occur on the maximum number of open operating system files and open DBMS cursors. If a maximum would be exceeded by opening a table, Uniface first temporarily closes one or more tables to release resources. During the execution of connector requests, the connector can dynamically open and close operating system files and DBMS cursors. The connector must inform Uniface about the number of files and cursors that it has opened or closed by calling the udcurscnt and udfilcnt service functions. The connector must inform Uniface about the maximum number of files that it can have open simultaneously for one open table, and about the maximum number of open cursors allowed by the DBMS. See uinfo and uctrl . If the connector supplies Uniface with correct information as described above, the resource management mechanisms are automatically handled by Uniface. Was this topic helpful?
